_note: Original memtrust fixture -- not derived from any published dataset. Modeled on mem0ai/mem0#4573 (GitHub user jamebobob), whose real 32-day production audit found 97.8% of 10,134 stored mem0 entries were junk the extraction pipeline should never have persisted, broken down as boot-file restating (~52.7%), cron/heartbeat noise (~11.5%), system dumps (~8.2%), hallucinated profiles (~5.2%), and a smaller remainder of other junk shapes. This fixture's `cases` array is small (15 hand-written items) and its per-category counts are chosen for coverage, not to statistically reproduce those exact percentages at 10,134-entry scale -- see docs/methodology.md for the honest caveat on what this fixture can and cannot prove. Each case carries a ground-truth `should_be_stored` label memtrust itself assigns (never read from any backend), a `category` tag, a `session_id` unique to that case so cases never contaminate each other's query results, and a `query` used to try to recall the stored content back out. The separate `feedback_loop_cases` array reproduces jamebobob's exact 808-duplicate mechanism: store a seed item, query it back (simulating recall into an agent's context), re-store that exact recalled text as new input, then check whether the matching record count grew by more than the single re-store call should have added. See evals/extraction_quality.py for the full scoring logic.
